profile.behavior: Diurnal, nonmigratory songbird that forages on or near the ground and in low shrubs, often in pairs; eats mostly seeds and fruit, adds insects, and feeds nestlings mainly insects. Both sexes sing clear whistles; breeding birds defend territories, while winter birds may gather in loose flocks.
field_marks: Prominent pointed crest on the crown
Short, very thick cone-shaped orange-red bill
Adult male bright scarlet red overall with black mask and throat
Adult female warm tan to olive-brown with reddish wings, tail, and crest
Long tail and compact, heavy-bodied songbird proportions
Dark eye set in the contrasting face pattern
profile.habitat: Dense shrubby cover in forest edges, hedgerows, overgrown fields, marshy thickets, parks, gardens, backyards, woodlots, and ornamental plantings; nests are usually hidden in dense foliage, shrubs, vines, or thickets.

sources.title: Northern Cardinal Identification, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Northern Cardinal Life History, All About Birds, Cornell Lab of Ornithology
Cardinalis cardinalis, Animal Diversity Web, University of Michigan Museum of Zoology

findings: Main male cardinal has correct one head, one beak, two legs, one tail, and no duplicated or malformed major body parts; the far wing is not separately visible in the side-view pose but no extra wing is present.
Plumage and field marks are strong: pointed crest, orange-red cone bill, black face/throat mask, scarlet male body, dark eye, long tail, and compact songbird proportions are represented.
Minor anatomy issue: the tail appears slightly long and narrow relative to the body, and the feet/toes are somewhat stylized, but not materially wrong for the species.
Visible text matches the expected common name, scientific name, family, measurements, habitat, behavior, field marks, and palette with no material factual errors observed.
No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears.
